changes_summary: Revised the route-aware design so capacity planning and locking are pool-scoped while route keys are used for demand attribution, backoff, and audit. The plan now preserves exactly one pool intent row per cycle, adds route-backoff state instead of independent route capacity planners, uses a pool lease for single-writer safety, and specifies CycleSummary counter integration for reconciler-driven terminations and suppressions.

success_criteria.criterion: The SQL migration creates `worker_capacity_intents` with the required columns/indexes, a route-backoff state table keyed by `(pool, route_key)`, and a TTL-based pool-scoped `orchestrator_leases` mechanism.
Migration registration and schema verification are wired into existing migration scripts or documented release commands, and shadow mode is gated on verified schema presence.
`CapacityReconciler` and supporting types/planning modules exist under `gpu_orchestrator/control/` and expose observe, plan, admit, pre-act record, act, and outcome record boundaries.
The reconciler writes exactly one pool summary row to `worker_capacity_intents` per pool per cycle; route backoff updates do not create extra pool intent rows.
The reconciler preserves route-scoped demand attribution and `(pool, route_key)` backoff while applying min floor, idle buffer, and max capacity only once at pool level.
Backoff on one route key does not suppress eligible demand for a different route key in the same pool, but all route demand still competes inside one pool capacity budget.
Multiple selected route keys cannot multiply `min_active_gpus`, `machines_to_keep_idle`, or `max_active_gpus`; pool capacity bounds are enforced once.
Authoritative mode enforces single-writer behavior per pool, not per route, so two reconciler instances cannot both execute side effects for the same pool.
`excluded_from_capacity_control` workers are neither counted nor mutated by capacity reconciliation, while periodic cleanup still receives excluded workers through the existing cleanup path.
The pure plan uses one pool desired-capacity formula with explicit precedence among min floor, eligible queued coverage, and busy-worker idle buffer.
Shadow mode records intent rows and shadow system_logs metadata but does not call RunPod, mutate worker rows, update backoff state, or acquire authoritative leases.
Shadow-mode control-loop ordering is post-lifecycle and pre-legacy-capacity, and this ordering is covered by tests.
Authoritative mode records an admitted pool intent successfully before executing any spawn, cancellation, or termination side effect.
RunPod spawn backoff is keyed by `(pool, route_key)` and tests verify 30s, 60s, 120s, 240s cooldowns plus reset on success using an injected fake clock.
Queued-zero replay/cycle tests prove a spawning worker is not cancelled merely because queued work momentarily drops to zero while active work remains in progress.
Cancel hysteresis tests verify cancellation only after the required wall-clock stability window across multiple snapshots or after `valid_until` elapses.
Route-stale workers are excluded from fresh selected-pool effective capacity and covered by tests.
Cutover adoption tests prove pre-existing spawning workers with no prior authoritative intent are counted as pool capacity and not cancelled on first authoritative observe.
Spawn idempotency is implemented by tagging initial worker rows with `capacity_intent_id` and action ordinal before RunPod launch, and duplicate action replay does not double-spawn.
`CancelPendingSpawn` attempts RunPod termination but marks the worker terminal with failure metadata even when RunPod termination fails, and this behavior is tested.

Reconciler-driven spawns, pending-spawn cancels, idle terminations, failed spawns, lease suppressions, and backoff suppressions are reflected in reconciler result counters and folded into `CycleSummary` where applicable.
The shadow, authoritative, and final cleanup slices each have explicit gates, and final deletion gates are not required for the initial shadow slice.
`_handle_early_termination` is removed in the final cleanup state, and `rg "_handle_early_termination"` returns zero matches.
`gpu_orchestrator/control/phases/` is removed in the final cleanup state and no production or test code imports `gpu_orchestrator.control.phases`.
`reigh-app/scripts/debug.py scaling-audit` is extended to include pool intent summaries, route backoff state, divergence, and intent stability without breaking import/compile.
Targeted reconciler, scaling, route-stale, route-aware task-count, pool-budget, summary-counter, and excluded-worker tests pass.
The full repository pytest suite passes after legacy phase tests are rewritten or removed.
The final design keeps lifecycle probing, promotion, health checks, stale route draining, error cleanup, orphan task reconciliation, and periodic zombie/storage checks intact.
The implementation avoids changing `system_logs.valid_source_type` and uses `orchestrator_gpu` with metadata tags for shadow intent logs.
Capacity planning code remains mostly pure and is small enough to reason about without hidden database or RunPod calls in PLAN/ADMIT.
Divergence audit SQL is clear enough for operators to inspect shadow-vs-legacy behavior using pool summaries plus route backoff details over the last 24h.
Production effective utilization reaches at least 70% sustained over 24h after cutover with no user-task failures attributable to the new controller.

assumptions: The architectural root cause remains the two-controller capacity loop; the latest critique corrects route budgeting and lock scope rather than replacing the approach.
Capacity limits are pool/orchestrator-level in this repository. Route keys are used for demand attribution, backoff, and audit, not for independent min/max capacity budgets.
The pool summary row in `worker_capacity_intents` is the required one-row-per-pool-per-cycle audit record; route backoff state is stored separately to satisfy `(pool, route_key)` cooldown semantics.
Route keys generally come from task-count route totals, not normal worker environment metadata. Existing workers without route-key metadata are still valid pool capacity.
Boot-time Railway schema mutation is intentionally avoided; migration application and schema verification are pre-deploy gates before enabling shadow mode.
The rollout remains split into shadow, authoritative cutover, and final cleanup slices. Final deletion acceptance criteria apply to the final cleanup slice, not the initial shadow slice.
The app debug command touch remains limited to the sibling `reigh-app` scaling-audit command.
